lesson.title: The Derivative
lesson.body: The derivative of f at x is defined as f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h, provided this limit exists. Geometrically, f'(a) is the slope of the tangent line to the curve y = f(x) at x = a. If f is differentiable at a, then f must also be continuous there — but the converse is false (e.g., f(x) = |x| at x = 0). The power rule provides the quickest path for polynomials: d/dx [xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹.

rooms.question: Which expression is the definition of f'(x)?
steps: Recall the limit definition of the derivative.
It measures the rate of change of f at x.
rooms.explanation: The derivative is defined as the limit of the difference quotient: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h.

rooms.question: Using the limit definition, find f'(x) for f(x) = x².
rooms.explanation: [( x+h)² - x²]/h = (2xh + h²)/h = 2x + h → 2x as h→0.

rooms.question: What is the slope of the tangent line to f(x) = 3x² - 4 at x = 2?
steps: Differentiate using the power rule: f'(x) = 6x.
rooms.explanation: f'(x) = 6x by the power rule. At x = 2: f'(2) = 6·2 = 12.

rooms.question: f(x) = |x| — is f differentiable at x = 0?
steps: Check the left-hand derivative: lim_{h→0⁻} (|0+h| - 0)/h = lim h<0: -h/h = -1.
Check the right-hand derivative: lim_{h→0⁺} h/h = 1.
answers: Yes, because f is continuous at 0
No — the left and right derivatives are unequal
No — f is not continuous at 0
rooms.explanation: Left derivative = -1, right derivative = 1. Since they differ, f'(0) does not exist — f is not differentiable at 0 despite being continuous there.

rooms.question: Order the steps to find the equation of the tangent line to f(x) = x³ at x = 1:
items: Evaluate f(1) = 1 to get the point (1, 1)
rooms.explanation: First differentiate to get f', then evaluate the slope at x=1, find the point, then apply point-slope form.

boss.name: The Difference Quotient
boss.taunt: I am the very foundation of calculus. Can you take my limit, or will you crumble in the face of h → 0?

questions.question: Use the limit definition to find f'(2) for f(x) = 5x - 3.
Apply the power rule: d/dx [x⁷]
questions.explanation: Power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹. So d/dx[x⁷] = 7x⁶.

questions.question: If f is differentiable at a, which must be true?
answers: f has a local extremum at a
questions.explanation: Differentiability implies continuity. The converse is false (e.g. |x| at 0 is continuous but not differentiable).
